did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a deep learning model, specifically a transfer learning-enhanced hybrid architecture based on EfficientNet-B3 with a Multi-Scale Attention Fusion Module (MSAFM), to classify melanoma and other skin lesions using the HAM10000 dermoscopic dataset. The study achieved 93.8% accuracy, a macro-F1 of 92.6%, and an AUC of 0.966.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a new 'transfer learning-enhanced hybrid architecture' (HEMSAF-Net) based on EfficientNet-B3 with a Multi-Scale Attention Fusion Module. It details a specific methodology including dataset preparation (HAM10000), model construction, and performance evaluation, reporting new empirical results (93.8% accuracy, macro-F1 92.6%, AUC 0.966) compared against baseline models. Although published in conference proceedings, it contains original algorithmic development and validation results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Skin Cancer Classification using a Transfer Learning–Enhanced Hybrid Architecture with Multi‑Scale Attention
journal: Proceedings of the 7th International Conference on Mobile Computing and Sustainable Informatics (ICMCSI-2026)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of melanoma classification from dermoscopic images by proposing a transfer learning-enhanced hybrid architecture named HEMSAF-Net. The model integrates an EfficientNet-B3 backbone with a Multi-Scale Attention Fusion Module (MSAFM) to improve feature extraction and contextual awareness. The authors utilized the HAM10000 dataset, which contains over 10,000 dermoscopic images across seven lesion types, applying class balancing strategies to handle dataset imbalance. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, macro-F1 score, and AUC-ROC, comparing the proposed model against baselines like ResNet50, InceptionV3, and EfficientNet-B3. The HEMSAF-Net achieved a superior accuracy of 93.8%, a macro-F1 of 92.6%, and an AUC of 0.966. These results indicate that the attention-enhanced hybrid architecture provides a robust and interpretable foundation for computer-assisted dermatological diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the seven lesion types in the HAM10000 dataset used for classification; described as precancerous spots.
One of the seven lesion types in the HAM10000 dataset; described as the most common form of skin cancer.
One of the seven lesion types in the HAM10000 dataset; covers seborrheic keratosis and solar lentigo.
One of the seven lesion types in the HAM10000 dataset; described as harmless, firm nodules.
One of the seven lesion types in the HAM10000 dataset; described as the most serious class. The primary focus of the study's classification task.
One of the seven lesion types in the HAM10000 dataset; common moles.
One of the seven lesion types in the HAM10000 dataset; includes angiomas and hemorrhages.

all_task_types.target: Seven discrete skin lesion classes (AKIEC, BCC, BKL, DF, MEL, NV, VASC) from the HAM10000 dataset.
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of dermoscopic images into seven specific diagnostic categories. The model predicts discrete lesion types using a transfer learning-enhanced hybrid architecture (HEMSAF-Net) based on EfficientNet-B3. Performance was evaluated using accuracy (93.8%), macro-F1 score (92.6%), and AUC-ROC (0.966). The task involves distinguishing between benign and malignant lesions as well as specific histopathological types.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, sourced from the ISIC archive. It contains images collected from multiple clinical centers with dermatologist-verified labels, many confirmed through histopathology.

use.training: Used for training the HEMSAF-Net model after data cleaning (removing duplicates and low-quality samples) and class balancing via oversampling.
use.validation: Used for monitoring validation loss to prevent overfitting and early stopping.
use.testing: Used for final performance evaluation (accuracy, macro-F1, AUC-ROC).
use.other: The dataset was partitioned using a stratified 70/15/15 split for training, validation, and testing respectively.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Partitioning the HAM10000 dataset into training (70%), validation (15%), and testing (15%) sets to ensure class distribution consistency across subsets.
To counteract class imbalance across lesion types during model training by assigning higher weights to underrepresented classes.
To evaluate model performance by giving equal weight to each lesion category, addressing the issue of skewed class distributions where accuracy can be misleading.

statistical_methods_used.method: AUC-ROC (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate model robustness against threshold variations in classification performance.
To provide visual explanations of model predictions and confirm that the model localized medically meaningful lesion regions, thereby assessing interpretability.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between the proposed HEMSAF-Net and baseline models (ResNet50, InceptionV3, EfficientNet-B3) were based on absolute metric values (Accuracy, Macro-F1, AUC-ROC) without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.evaluation_setting: Multi-class classification of seven skin lesion types (AKIEC, BCC, BKL, DF, MEL, NV, VASC)
HAM10000.notes: The proposed HEMSAF-Net achieved the highest performance across all metrics compared to baselines. Most classification errors occurred in lesion categories with overlapping or ambiguous visual characteristics, specifically between melanoma and nevus, and between BCC and dermatofibroma. Grad-CAM visualizations confirmed the model localized medically meaningful lesion regions.

architecture_used.main_model: HEMSAF-Net (Transfer Learning-Enhanced Hybrid Architecture with Multi-Scale Attention)
architecture_used.pretraining: Pretrained weights from EfficientNet-B3 (and ResNet50 for baseline comparison) were used via transfer learning and fine-tuning.

msafm_components.channel_attention_details: Uses global average pooling and global max pooling, followed by two trainable weight matrices (W1, W2) with ReLU and Sigmoid activations.
msafm_components.spatial_attention_details: Uses 7x7 convolution on concatenated average and max pooled features, followed by Sigmoid activation.

architecture_details.compound_scaling: EfficientNet compound scaling (depth, width, resolution) applied to backbone

training_details.class_balancing: Oversampling of underrepresented classes; class weights calculated as N/Nc

preprocessing_techniques: Standard normalization (Inorm = (I - µ) / σ, where µ is dataset mean and σ is dataset standard deviation)
Artifact reduction using BlackHat filtering for hair and illumination noise removal (H = BH(I) = (I.S) – I, where S is a structuring element for morphological closing)
Data augmentation including rotation (±20 degrees), flipping, color perturbation, and scaling

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied with angles in the range of [-20, 20] degrees.
Geometric transform applied to increase dataset diversity.
Photometric transform involving contrast and brightness shifts.
Geometric transform applied to increase dataset diversity.
Synthetic data generation technique used to enhance generalization and mitigate imbalance.
Class balancing augmentation strategy applied to underrepresented classes in the HAM10000 dataset.

conclusions: The proposed HEMSAF-Net model, which integrates a Multi-Scale Attention Fusion Module with EfficientNet-B3, achieved superior performance on the HAM10000 dataset with 93.8% accuracy, a macro-F1 score of 92.6%, and an AUC of 0.966.

The attention-enhanced hybrid architecture provides a robust and interpretable foundation for computer-assisted dermatological diagnosis by strengthening contextual awareness and emphasizing clinically important patterns.
Grad-CAM visualizations confirmed that the model localized medically meaningful lesion regions, reinforcing the interpretability of the attention module.

Most classification errors occurred in lesion categories with overlapping or ambiguous visual characteristics, specifically between melanoma and nevus, and between basal cell carcinoma and dermatofibroma, which are also difficult for dermatologists to differentiate.
The study acknowledges that while the model produces high accuracy, it is intended to complement the diagnostic process rather than replace trained clinicians.
Clinical deployment must account for variations in imaging equipment, patient populations, and acquisition protocols across different institutions.
Broader external validation is essential before the system can be applied in real-world settings.

Future work will focus on improving dataset diversity, conducting robustness testing, enhancing model transparency, exploring multi-task learning, integrating patient metadata, and optimizing for on-device portable diagnostic tools.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which introduces potential population and geographic bias as the data originates from specific clinical centers, limiting generalizability to diverse global populations.
No external validation was performed; the authors explicitly state that broader external validation is essential before the system can be applied in real-world clinical settings.

The paper does not report any statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for the performance differences between the proposed HEMSAF-Net and baseline models, relying solely on absolute metric comparisons.

Classification errors were concentrated in clinically ambiguous categories with overlapping visual features, specifically between melanoma and nevus, and between basal cell carcinoma and dermatofibroma, which are also difficult for dermatologists to differentiate.

The model is intended to complement, not replace, trained clinicians, and deployment must account for variations in imaging equipment, patient populations, and acquisition protocols across different institutions.

The dataset was naturally imbalanced; while class balancing strategies (oversampling and class-weighted loss) were used, the reliance on synthetic balancing may not fully reflect real-world prevalence distributions.
The paper is a conference proceeding published in 2026, which may indicate preliminary results or limited peer-review depth compared to high-impact journal publications.
